title_en: Zakat and Sawm: Their Conditions and Rulings
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa: Syarat-Syarat dan Ketentuan-Ketentuannya
content_en:
Ibn Qudamah's treatment of zakah in Al-Kafi demonstrates his characteristic approach of combining precision in ruling-statement with clarity in evidential grounding. zakah is obligatory upon every free Muslim who owns wealth reaching the nisab (threshold) and who has held that wealth for a full lunar year (hawl), with the exception of agricultural produce and minerals, for which the hawl is not a condition.

The categories of zakatable wealth in Al-Kafi follow the standard Hanbali taxonomy: gold and silver (and by extension, cash and monetary instruments), trade goods valued against the silver nisab, grains and fruits subject to zakah (wheat, barley, dates, raisins, and by scholarly extension, any major staple crop), livestock of three types (camels, cattle, and small ruminants), and buried treasure or mineral wealth (rikaz and ma'dan).

Ibn Qudamah examines the nisab for each category with reference to prophetic reports. For gold: 20 mithqal (roughly 85 grams). For silver: 200 dirhams (roughly 595 grams). The rate is one-quarter of a tenth (2.5%). For agricultural produce: the nisab is 5 awsuq (approximately 653 kilograms of the staple product), and the rate is 10% if rain-fed and 5% if irrigated — based on the authentic hadith: 'On that which is watered by the sky and springs, a tenth; and on that watered by irrigation, a twentieth' (al-Bukhari).

For livestock, Ibn Qudamah presents the detailed tables transmitted from the Prophet's practice. For camels: no zakah below five; one sheep is due for every five up to twenty-five; then the scale shifts to young camels. For cattle: one yearling male (tabi') for every thirty head, and a two-year-old female (musinnah) for every forty. For sheep and goats: one sheep per forty head up to one hundred twenty, then the scale increases.

The eight categories of zakah recipients (asnaf) are enumerated with their definitions. Ibn Qudamah emphasizes that the fuqara' (the poor) and masakin (the destitute) are the primary recipients, and that paying zakah to relatives who are not in one's financial dependency is especially meritorious because it combines the reward of zakah with the reward of maintaining family ties (silat ar-rahim).

The chapter on sawm (fasting) covers the Ramadan fast in detail. The fast begins upon verified sighting of the new crescent moon or completion of thirty days of Sha'ban. Ibn Qudamah discusses the scholarly question of whether local moon-sighting or a global sighting applies, presenting the Hanbali view that one region's sighting is sufficient to establish the fast for the entire Muslim community if it is reliably reported.

The fast's nullifiers are listed: intentional eating and drinking, sexual intercourse (which additionally requires kaffarah), intentional vomiting, cupping (hijamah — the Hanbali position based on the hadith of Shaddad ibn Aws), and the onset of menstruation or postnatal bleeding. Ibn Qudamah notes the evidential debates around some of these, particularly the cupping ruling, giving the student a sense of how Hanbali positions relate to the underlying hadith evidence.
content_id:
Pembahasan Ibn Qudamah mengenai zakat dalam Al-Kafi menunjukkan pendekatannya yang khas dalam menggabungkan presisi pernyataan ketentuan dengan kejelasan landasan pembuktian. Zakat wajib bagi setiap Muslim merdeka yang memiliki harta mencapai nisab (ambang batas) dan yang telah memiliki harta tersebut selama satu tahun Hijriah penuh (haul), dengan pengecualian hasil pertanian dan mineral, di mana haul bukan merupakan syarat.

Kategori-kategori kekayaan yang dikenai zakat dalam Al-Kafi mengikuti taksonomi Hanbali standar: emas dan perak (dan dengan perluasan, uang tunai dan instrumen moneter), barang dagangan yang dinilai berdasarkan nisab perak, biji-bijian dan buah-buahan yang dikenai zakat (gandum, gandum kasar, kurma, kismis, dan dengan perluasan ilmiah, tanaman pokok utama mana pun), hewan ternak tiga jenis (unta, sapi, dan hewan berkaki kecil), serta harta terpendam atau kekayaan mineral (rikaz dan ma'dan).

Ibn Qudamah meneliti nisab untuk setiap kategori dengan merujuk pada riwayat-riwayat kenabian. Untuk emas: 20 mitsqal (sekitar 85 gram). Untuk perak: 200 dirham (sekitar 595 gram). Tarifnya adalah seperempat dari sepersepuluh (2,5%). Untuk hasil pertanian: nisabnya adalah 5 wasaq (sekitar 653 kilogram produk pokok), dan tarifnya adalah 10% jika disirami hujan dan 5% jika diairi — berdasarkan hadis sahih: 'Pada yang disiram oleh langit dan mata air, sepersepuluh; dan pada yang diairi dengan irigasi, seperduapuluh' (Bukhari).

Untuk hewan ternak, Ibn Qudamah menyajikan tabel-tabel terperinci yang diturunkan dari praktik kenabian. Untuk unta: tidak ada zakat di bawah lima ekor; satu kambing wajib untuk setiap lima ekor hingga dua puluh lima; kemudian skalanya beralih ke unta muda. Untuk sapi: satu jantan setahun (tabi') untuk setiap tiga puluh ekor, dan satu betina berusia dua tahun (musinnah) untuk setiap empat puluh. Untuk kambing dan domba: satu kambing per empat puluh ekor hingga seratus dua puluh, kemudian skala meningkat.

Delapan asnaf penerima zakat dihitung beserta definisinya. Ibn Qudamah menekankan bahwa fuqara' (orang miskin) dan masakin (orang yang sangat membutuhkan) adalah penerima utama, dan bahwa membayar zakat kepada kerabat yang tidak berada dalam tanggungan finansial seseorang sangat utama karena menggabungkan pahala zakat dengan pahala memelihara ikatan keluarga (silaturahmi).

Bab tentang puasa mencakup puasa Ramadan secara terperinci. Puasa dimulai setelah rukyat hilal baru yang terverifikasi atau genap tiga puluh hari Sya'ban. Ibn Qudamah membahas persoalan ilmiah mengenai apakah rukyat lokal atau rukyat global yang berlaku, menyajikan pandangan Hanbali bahwa rukyat satu wilayah cukup untuk menetapkan puasa bagi seluruh komunitas Muslim jika dilaporkan secara dapat dipercaya.

Pembatal-pembatal puasa didaftar: makan dan minum dengan sengaja, hubungan seksual (yang selain itu juga mensyaratkan kafarat), muntah yang disengaja, berbekam (hijamah — posisi Hanbali berdasarkan hadis Syaddad ibn Aws), serta mulainya haid atau nifas. Ibn Qudamah mencatat perdebatan pembuktian di sekitar beberapa dari ini, khususnya ketentuan berbekam, memberikan pelajar gambaran tentang bagaimana posisi-posisi Hanbali berkaitan dengan bukti hadis yang mendasarinya.
content_ar: Ibn Qudamah's treatment of الزكاة in Al-Kafi demonstrates his characteristic approach of combining precision in ruling-statement with clarity in evidential grounding. الزكاة is obligatory upon every free مسلم who owns الثروة reaching the nisab (threshold) and who has held that الثروة for a full lunar year (hawl), with the exception of agricultural produce and minerals, for which the hawl is not a condition. The categories of zakatable الثروة in Al-Kafi follow the standard الحنبلي taxonomy: gold and silver (and by extension, cash and monetary instruments), trade goods valued against the silver nisab, grains and fruits subject to الزكاة (wheat, barley, dates, raisins, and by العلمي extension, any major staple crop), livestock of three types (camels, cattle, and small ruminants), and buried treasure or mineral الثروة (rikaz and ma'dan). Ibn Qudamah examines the nisab for each category with reference to prophetic reports. For gold: 20 mithqal (roughly 85 grams). For silver: 200 dirhams (roughly 595 grams). The rate is one-quarter of a عاشراً (2.5%). For agricultural produce: the nisab is 5 awsuq (approximately 653 kilograms of the staple product), and the rate is 10% إذا rain-fed and 5% إذا irrigated — based on the authentic الحديث: 'On that which is watered by the sky and springs, a عاشراً; and on that watered by irrigation, a twentieth' (البخاري). For livestock, Ibn Qudamah presents the detailed tables transmitted from النبي ﷺ's practice. For camels: no الزكاة below five; one sheep is due for every five up to twenty-five; then the scale shifts to young camels. For cattle: one yearling male (tabi') for every thirty head, and a two-year-old female (musinnah) for every forty. For sheep and goats: one sheep per forty head up to one hundred twenty, then the scale increases. The eight categories of الزكاة recipients (asnaf) are enumerated with their definitions. Ibn Qudamah emphasizes that the fuqara' (the poor) and masakin (the destitute) are the الأساسي recipients, and that paying الزكاة to relatives who are not in one's financial dependency is ولا سيما meritorious لأن it combines the الأجر of الزكاة with the الأجر of maintaining الأسرة ties (silat ar-rahim). The الباب on الصيام (الصيام) covers the Ramadan fast in detail. The fast begins upon verified sighting of the new crescent moon or completion of thirty days of Sha'ban. Ibn Qudamah discusses the العلمي question of whether local moon-sighting or a global sighting applies, presenting the الحنبلي view that one region's sighting is sufficient to establish the fast for the entire مسلم الجماعة إذا it is reliably reported. The fast's nullifiers are listed: intentional eating and drinking, sexual intercourse (which additionally requires kaffarah), intentional vomiting, cupping (hijamah — the الحنبلي position based on the الحديث of Shaddad ibn Aws), and the onset of menstruation or postnatal bleeding.
